quote_of_day.text:
>>58494
>chandu from andher nagari, bihari colony is complaining about reliance industries' r&d spend
LMAO. imagine being this far gone. this guy is hopeless. if he hasn't offed himself already, what's to say that his shameless self ever will ... one wonders.
quote_of_day.name: Anon

articles.title: Weather on the board today
articles.body:
A high-pressure system of caste debate settled over the board, with a dominant front of discussion around a hypothetical homogenous Indian society. This brought scattered squalls of opinion, with some posters weighing in on the potential benefits and drawbacks.

A side current of genetic discussion blew in, as an inquiry into the origins of ancient steppe ancestry in India drew some responses. The conversation remained relatively clear, with posters sharing their takes on the similarities between Indian and European genetics.

A brief but intense downpour of criticism hit the board, focused on a movie that drew the ire of at least one poster. Insults were hurled at the cast and crew, with only one actor spared.

Meanwhile, a light drizzle of satellite reminders continued, with one poster noting that India has a satellite in orbit called SCAT-SAT-1.

A cold snap of celebrity ridicule also moved in, as Sidharth Malhotra's looks were mocked and an unfounded claim about his rumored relationship with Karan Johar circulated.

articles.title: Anon Ponders a Homogenous Indian Society
articles.body:
Anon opened a discussion on the potential benefits and drawbacks of India becoming a homogeneous society without caste. One anon countered that certain lower castes, such as Bhangis and Chamars, provide essential sanitation work, which is often lacking.

They argued that these groups are not necessarily opposed to manual scavenging, and that many still perform such tasks.

Another anon shared an anecdote about a lynching incident where Hindu organizations intervened to help a Dalit victim, suggesting that these organizations can be a positive force.

However, they also acknowledged that some lower castes can be problematic, citing issues like illiteracy and criminal behavior.

A respondent argued that the focus on caste-based groups can be misguided, and that it's essential to stop thinking in terms of caste-based first-response groups and organizations.

They posited that these groups can be more of a mafia and a nuisance to law and order than an asset.

One anon pointed out that manual scavenging is still a reality for many Dalits, despite claims that it's a dying profession. They shared a personal observation about seeing Dalits working with modern trucks and machines in metropolitan cities.

Anon also discussed the relationship between lower castes and Muslims, with some arguing that Muslims frequently harm Dalits, which strengthens their point about the need for social cohesion.

Others emphasized that dominant castes still hold power and that social dynamics are more complex than a simple 'war' between groups.

The discussion also touched on the idea that a homogeneous society would not necessarily lead to ethnic wars, as there would be no significant differences in lineage or status between castes.

However, some anons disputed this, citing historical and contemporary tensions between groups.

Throughout the conversation, anons expressed a range of opinions on the role of caste in Indian society, the responsibilities of different groups, and the potential consequences of a homogeneous society.

No clear consensus emerged, but the discussion highlighted the complexity of these issues.

articles.title: Ancient Steppe Ancestry in India
articles.body:
Anon questioned the origin of steppe ancestry in India, suggesting it could be native or very ancient.

They noted genetic similarities with Europeans and argued that the widespread presence of steppe ancestry in South India implies an ancient arrival.

Another anon countered that the uncertainty around the origin of 'steppe' ancestry is a major issue, suggesting that it's possible all haplogroups are native and that interactions with steppe populations could have occurred.

This anon also criticized the reliance on theories that may change every decade.

Anon brought up the study of R1a, a haplogroup associated with steppe ancestry, noting that while its distribution is well-documented, its origin remains unclear, with some sources citing 'Eurasia' as a possible place of origin.

Anon disputed the idea that steppe ancestry was present in the Indus Valley Civilization (IVC), citing the lack of steppe DNA in IVC samples.

However, another anon claimed that some steppe DNA was found in Rakhigarhi samples, albeit in low amounts.

The discussion also touched on the presence of steppe DNA in Indian populations, with some anons arguing that it is primarily found in paternal lines, while others suggested that there may be some matrilineal steppe DNA present in trace amounts.

Anons exchanged requests and claims about the existence of papers and information supporting these claims, with one anon describing the current understanding as 'mostly a hypothesis'.

articles.title: Criticism of a Movie
articles.body:
Anon called a movie 'shit', criticizing its length, pretentiousness, and cast, except for Robert Pattinson. They also disliked the end credit song by Travis Scott. Anon expressed discontent with the movie, describing it as boring, long, and pretentious.

They criticized the cast and actors, except for Robert Pattinson, although they noted that Pattinson has started to look unusual, possibly due to jawline implants.

Another anon asked how much was paid for a ticket, and a separate anon mentioned obtaining something through their agency, without specifying what it was.

articles.title: Anon reminds India has SCAT-SAT-1 satellite in orbit
articles.body: Anon posted a daily reminder that India has a satellite in orbit called SCAT-SAT-1. Another anon responded with a link to the Wikipedia page for ScatSat-1, saying 'inb4' and referencing a possible misspelling. The conversation quickly devolved into jokes and insults, with one anon calling out a 'poser musalaman' claiming to be someone else. Anon also made a comment about 'SCAT' being an 'aryan kink'.

articles.title: Anon mocks Sidharth Malhotra's looks, claims he slept with Karan Johar
articles.body: Anon posted a list of outlandish claims about Sidharth Malhotra, including that he was 'forced to buttfuck Karan Johar to get movies.' One respondent joked that Sidharth Malhotra's height is his only redeeming quality, comparing him unfavorably to someone who achieved 12% body fat. Another anon claimed that 'looksmaxxers' – individuals focused on physical appearance – are often 'psychos' who repel women despite being conventionally attractive.
